title: A Single Twist-Angle Selection Method for the Electronic Structure of Bilayer Materials
authors: Ryan A. Baker, William Z. Van Benschoten, James J. Shepherd
abstract: Structure factor twist averaging (sfTA) is a newer method that has been shown to reproduce twist-averaged (TA) CCSD energies for bulk systems at a low computational cost. In this work, we extend this method for the treatment of low-dimensional materials in the form of two variants: paired sfTA and binding sfTA. These variants affect which twist angles are used in the sfTA protocol, as well as how the special twist angle is selected, namely by using the binding structure factor. These changes are meant to incorporate the binding interaction into the twist-angle selection algorithm within sfTA. Both variants are tested on a variety of bilayer systems, and the resulting binding correlation energies are compared to original sfTA results. We show that the variants are able to produce results approaching TA, with binding sfTA producing the most accurate energies. We also use contour plots of the test systems to show that these improvements are most likely caused by a cancellation of errors.
summary: This paper introduces new variants of the Structure Factor Twist Averaging (sfTA) method designed to efficiently calculate the electronic structure of bilayer materials. By using a 'binding' approach to select a single twist angle for both layers, the authors significantly reduce the error in interlayer binding energy calculations. The new method maintains the massive computational savings of the original sfTA while approaching the accuracy of much more expensive twist-averaging protocols. This is particularly important for studying the correlation energies of 2D materials like graphene and transition metal dichalcogenides.
main_problem: The high computational cost of using twist-angle averaging (TA) to reduce finite-size errors in the electronic structure calculations of bilayer materials.
main_result: The development of 'paired sfTA' and 'binding sfTA' variants significantly improves the accuracy of interlayer binding correlation energies, with 'binding sfTA' achieving results approaching the true twist-averaged limit.
method: The authors extend the Structure Factor Twist Averaging (sfTA) method by using low-level MP2 calculations to select a single 'special twist angle' for high-level CCSD calculations, specifically tailoring the selection process to include the binding transition structure factor.
model_or_system: The study focuses on low-dimensional bilayer systems, including various 2D materials such as graphene, BN, SiC, MoS2, and MoSe2, primarily in AB stacking configurations.
key_observables: Interlayer binding correlation energy, monolayer and bilayer correlation energies, and the transition structure factor S(G).
important_parameters: Twist angle/offset, k-mesh density, and the mean absolute error (MAE) relative to the twist-averaged (TA) reference.
main_assumptions: The method assumes that the transition structure factor at the selected special twist angle accurately approximates the twist-averaged value, and that MP2 can serve as a reliable intermediate for CCSD calculations.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the construction of the binding transition structure factor; Figure 2 uses box plots to compare MAE across sfTA variants; Figure 5 compares binding correlation energies for various systems; Figure 6 provides contour plots of energy landscapes in graphene to demonstrate error cancellation.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of finite-size errors in bilayers, proposes two new variants of the sfTA method (paired and binding), evaluates their accuracy against TA benchmarks across multiple material systems, and provides a physical explanation for the improved accuracy via error cancellation in energy landscapes.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
